content.text: The choice of interpolation nodes profoundly affects both the accuracy and stability of polynomial interpolation. Equally-spaced nodes lead to Runge's phenomenon for certain functions. **Chebyshev nodes** provide an optimal remedy, minimizing the maximum interpolation error.

content.title: Runge's Phenomenon
content.content:
For the function $f(x) = \frac{1}{1 + 25x^2}$ on $[-1, 1]$ with $n+1$ equally-spaced nodes $x_k = -1 + \frac{2k}{n}$, the polynomial interpolation error satisfies:
\[
\lim_{n \to \infty} \max_{x \in [-1,1]} |P_n(x) - f(x)| = \infty
\]
The interpolating polynomials diverge despite $f$ being smooth.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Chebyshev Polynomials of the First Kind
content.content:
The **Chebyshev polynomial** $T_n(x)$ is defined for $x \in [-1, 1]$ by:
$$

T_n(x) = \cos(n \arccos x), \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
$$

content.title: Properties of Chebyshev Polynomials
content.content:
1. $T_n(x)$ is a polynomial of degree exactly $n$

2. $|T_n(x)| \le 1$ for $x \in [-1, 1]$

3. $T_n(x) = 2xT_{n-1}(x) - T_{n-2}(x)$ for $n \ge 2$ (recurrence)

4. $T_n(x)$ has $n$ simple zeros in $(-1, 1)$

5. Leading coefficient: $T_n(x) = 2^{n-1}x^n + \text{lower terms}$ for $n \ge 1$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
(1) Set $x = \cos\theta$. Then $T_n(\cos\theta) = \cos(n\theta)$. Using the identity:
\[
\cos(n\theta) = 2\cos\theta \cos((n-1)\theta) - \cos((n-2)\theta)
\]
we get the recurrence $T_n(x) = 2xT_{n-1}(x) - T_{n-2}(x)$ with $T_0(x) = 1$, $T_1(x) = x$.

This shows $T_n$ is a polynomial. Induction on the recurrence shows $\deg T_n = n$.

(2) Since $|\cos(n\arccos x)| \le 1$ for all $x \in [-1, 1]$.

(3) The recurrence relation above.

(4) $T_n(x) = 0$ iff $\cos(n\arccos x) = 0$ iff $n\arccos x = (2k+1)\frac{\pi}{2}$ for integer $k$. This gives $x = \cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{2n}$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, n-1$: exactly $n$ zeros.

(5) From the recurrence: if $T_{n-1}$ has leading coefficient $2^{n-2}$, then $T_n = 2x \cdot 2^{n-2}x^{n-1} - \cdots$ has leading coefficient $2^{n-1}$.

content.title: Chebyshev Nodes
content.content:
The **Chebyshev nodes** (or **Chebyshev points**) of the first kind are the zeros of $T_{n+1}(x)$:
$$

x_k = \cos\left(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{2(n+1)}\right), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, n
$$

The **Chebyshev nodes of the second kind** (or **Chebyshev-Lobatto points**) include the endpoints:
$$

x_k = \cos\left(\frac{k\pi}{n}\right), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, n
$$
These are the extrema of $T_n(x)$.

content.title: Minimax Property of Chebyshev Polynomials
content.content:
Among all monic polynomials of degree $n$, the polynomial $\frac{1}{2^{n-1}}T_n(x)$ has the smallest maximum absolute value on $[-1, 1]$:
$$

\min_{\substack{p \text{ monic} \\ \deg p = n}} \max_{x \in [-1,1]} |p(x)| = \frac{1}{2^{n-1}}
$$
The minimum is achieved uniquely by $\frac{1}{2^{n-1}}T_n(x)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $\tilde{T}_n(x) = \frac{1}{2^{n-1}}T_n(x)$, the monic Chebyshev polynomial. Then $|\tilde{T}_n(x)| \le \frac{1}{2^{n-1}}$ on $[-1, 1]$.

Moreover, $\tilde{T}_n$ attains the values $\pm\frac{1}{2^{n-1}}$ at $n+1$ points: the extrema of $T_n$ at $x_k = \cos\frac{k\pi}{n}$ for $k = 0, \ldots, n$, with alternating signs.

Suppose $p(x)$ is another monic polynomial of degree $n$ with $\|p\|_\infty < \frac{1}{2^{n-1}}$. Consider $q(x) = \tilde{T}_n(x) - p(x)$. This has degree $< n$ (the leading terms cancel).

At the $n+1$ alternating extrema of $\tilde{T}_n$, the sign of $\tilde{T}_n$ alternates while $|p| < |\tilde{T}_n|$. Thus $q$ has the same sign as $\tilde{T}_n$ at these points, meaning $q$ alternates in sign at $n+1$ points. By the Intermediate Value Theorem, $q$ has at least $n$ zeros.

But $\deg q < n$, which is a contradiction. Hence no such $p$ exists.

The node polynomial $\omega(x) = \prod_{k=0}^{n}(x-x_k)$ is monic of degree $n+1$. By Theorem [thm:chebyshev-minimax], its minimum max-norm is achieved when $\omega = \frac{1}{2^n}T_{n+1}$, i.e., when the nodes are zeros of $T_{n+1}$.

content.title: Interpolation Error with Chebyshev Nodes
content.content:
Let $f \in C^{n+1}[-1,1]$ and let $P_n$ be the interpolating polynomial at the Chebyshev nodes. Then:
$$

\|f - P_n\|_\infty \le \frac{M_{n+1}}{2^n (n+1)!}
$$
where $M_{n+1} = \|f^{(n+1)}\|_\infty$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
By the interpolation error formula:
\[
|f(x) - P_n(x)| \le \frac{M_{n+1}}{(n+1)!} |\omega_n(x)|
\]

With Chebyshev nodes, $\omega_n = \frac{1}{2^n}T_{n+1}$, and $|T_{n+1}(x)| \le 1$.

content.text: For interpolation on $[a, b]$ instead of $[-1, 1]$, use the linear transformation:

content.title: Chebyshev Nodes on $[a,b
content.content:
$]
The Chebyshev nodes on $[a, b]$ are:
$$

x_k = \frac{b-a}{2}\cos\left(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{2(n+1)}\right) + \frac{a+b}{2}, \quad k = 0, \ldots, n
$$

content.title: Chebyshev interpolation of Runge's function
content.problem:
Interpolate $f(x) = \frac{1}{1+25x^2}$ on $[-1, 1]$ using $n+1$ Chebyshev nodes.

| $n$ | Equally-spaced error | Chebyshev error |
| 5 | 0.43 | 0.11 |
| 10 | 1.0 | 0.016 |
| 15 | 5.2 | 0.0022 |
| 20 | 60 | 0.00030 |
| 30 | $> 10^4$ | $5.5 \times 10^{-6}$ |

With Chebyshev nodes, the error decreases geometrically rather than exploding.

content.title: Chebyshev Expansion
content.content:
Any function $f \in C[-1,1]$ can be expanded as:
$$

f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} c_k T_k(x)
$$
where the **Chebyshev coefficients** are:
$$

c_k = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{f(x)T_k(x)}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx \quad (c_0 \text{ has factor } 1/\pi)
$$

content.title: Convergence of Chebyshev Series
content.content:
If $f$ is analytic on $[-1,1]$ and can be extended analytically to an ellipse with foci at $\pm 1$, then its Chebyshev series converges geometrically:
\[
|c_k| = O(\rho^{-k})
\]
where $\rho > 1$ depends on the size of the ellipse of analyticity.
content.label: Theorem
